title: Senator Dave Cortese introduced legislation today that will help communities prepare for sudden closures of psychiatric and maternity wards in hospitals. SB 1300 responds to a ment

Return concise, standalone claim_texts that could be tested against scientific literature. Exclude purely procedural statem

raw_text:
```json
{
  "claims": [
    "California has witnessed an alarming rate of maternity ward closures, with at least 46 units shutting down over the past twelve years.",
    "Over half of the maternity ward closures in California occurred in the last three years.",
    "The closure of maternity wards disproportionately impacts low-income, Black, and Latino communities.",
    "Vulnerable populations in California are at risk due to significant healthcare challenges.",
    "California confronts a severe shortage of mental health beds.",
    "The success of the Community Assistance, Recovery, and Empowerment (CARE) Court program hinges on the availability of mental health facilities.",
    "The closure of vital psychiatric and maternity units can create a public health crisis.",
    "Sudden hospital closures in lower-income areas result in what can be described as 21st-century redlining, cutting underrepresented people off from essential services.",
    "SB 1300 requires hospitals to provide a notice period for the closure of maternity or psychiatric units extending from 90 to 120 days.",
    "SB 1300 compels hospitals to submit a comprehensive report detailing the potential impact of unit closures on the community.",
    "The sudden closure of psychiatric facilities creates longer wait times, negatively impacting individuals in crisis.",
    "Bed shortages due to facility closures increase stress and financial burdens on families supporting individuals with mental illness.",
    "Senator Cortese supported the 2023-24 state budget, which allocated nearly $3 billion to primary care, including significant investments in behavioral health capacity."
  ]
}
```
